elements.sent-2411-001: Different populations of vehicles travel along a network.
elements.sent-2411-002: Each population has its origin, destination and travel costs --- which may well be unbounded.
elements.sent-2411-003: Under the only requirement of the continuity of the travel costs, we prove the existence of a Nash equilibrium for all populations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Conditions for its uniqueness are also provided.
elements.sent-2411-005: A few cases are treated in detail to show specific situations of interest.
elements.sent-2411-006: Keywords: Nash Equilibria on Traffic Networks; Multi-Populations Braess Paradox; Multi-Populations Models on Networks AMS 2020 Mathematics Subject Classification: 90B20, 91A80, 91B74.
elements.sent-2411-007: A road network is used by different vehicles, grouped into populations according to their origin, their destination and their travel costs.
elements.sent-2411-008: For each population, the origin is connected to the destination by different routes, each consisting of a sequence of adjacent roads.
elements.sent-2411-009: Traveling along a road bears a cost, in general different for each population, which may be related to gas consumption, pollutant production or travel time.
elements.sent-2411-010: In the latter case, it is reasonable to admit that it can become infinite, for instance when congestions occur.
elements.sent-2411-011: A road can be shared by drivers from various populations, with travel costs varying according to the level of congestion.
elements.sent-2411-012: Each traveler chooses a route among those available to its population and, correspondingly, pays a route cost which is the sum of the costs to its population of the roads constituting that route.
elements.sent-2411-013: It is then natural to consider several concurrent and interdependent games, each played by the vehicles in a single population.
elements.sent-2411-014: According to the selfish paradigm~\cite{Roughgarden2004332, Roughgarden2005, Roughgarden200093 , each individual chooses a route such that no different choice could be more convenient.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In other words, all populations distribute among the different routes so that each game is at a Nash equilibrium and the whole network is at a global Nash equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-016: Below, under rather general assumptions, we prove the existence of such global Nash equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-017: At these configurations, for each population, all vehicles in that population pay the same cost.
elements.sent-2411-018: Moreover, at the equilibria we exhibit, no individual, whatever the population it belongs to, finds convenient to change its route choice.
elements.sent-2411-019: The costs of different populations may well be unrelated, in the sense that a population can be interested in minimizing travel time, another in gas consumption, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the case of a single population, frameworks essentially equivalent to the one presented below are classical, see for instance~\cite{MR247935, MR801491, Nagurney2007355, Roughgarden2005, Roughgarden200093 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In general, existence proofs of Nash equilibria rely on the considered game being a potential game, see~\cite{beckmann, MR247935 , a property that here fails due to the presence of multiple populations.}
elements.sent-2411-022: A different approach to multiple populations competing on the same network is presented in~\cite{MR3337989 , where a measure based framework is employed.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Nash equilibria in multi-agent systems are considered, for instance, in~\cite{MR3950676 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: As the number of populations and the complexity increase, it is realistic to expect that multiple (global) Nash equilibria may occur.
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus, conditions ensuring uniqueness in general situations become more intricate and require stricter assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-026: First, we present an equality satisfied by all (global) Nash equilibria.
elements.sent-2411-027: As long as the number of populations is small and the networks are simple, this condition may ensure uniqueness, as examples show.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the Appendix, we also present a general uniqueness theorem.

elements.sent-2411-146: In this example two different populations, the hat and the check population, move from different origins to the same destination.
elements.sent-2411-147: Our aim is to show that the addition of a road, aimed at helping the hat population, has a negative effect on both the hat and the check population.
elements.sent-2411-148: In more details, consider the situation~\eqref{eq:49 , where $N=5$, $\widehat n = 2$, $\widecheck n = 2$ and all roads are one way downwards.}
